title: Open Workshop: Feeders, Team Structure, Design-Build Strategy, and Project Close-Out
summary: Participants brought live business challenges to Erin and the group, covering topics including how to identify and build relationships with architect and designer feeders, how to structure and scale a project management team for spec home building, the strategic trade-offs of committing to a design-build model, and best practices for closing out projects and off-boarding difficult clients. Erin drew on her own journey from small remodels to high-end custom homes to ground her advice in real experience.

teachings.explanation: Every market has key referral sources—architects, designers, or others—who feed high-quality clients to builders. Identifying and deliberately cultivating relationships with these feeders over time is the primary path to moving into higher-end, better-fit work. This process can take years and requires persistence.
teachings.quote: I make a list I'd still do this this year every year I have this list it will take me sometimes four years to get into the right architect four years I look back at my lists right so it's not a quick process but you have to know who that list is.

teachings.explanation: Builders must define their target market and goals clearly before deciding which projects to take. Without this clarity, you end up chasing revenue, taking misaligned work, and burning out. Once goals are set, it becomes easier and healthier to decline projects that don't fit.
teachings.quote: The more that happens you'll be saying no nine times out of ten like it's nice that they're coming to you but you've got to be really clear on where you're going.

teachings.explanation: Remodeling is often undervalued by builders who aspire to new construction, but it offers better margins and is harder to do well, creating a competitive moat. Excellence in remodeling is a legitimate and lucrative path that can lead organically to larger work.
teachings.quote: Don't discount remodeling. Remodeling is the margins are better it's much harder as you know and not a lot of people like them or are good at them that's a huge market if you excel in remodeling.

teachings.concept: Project Managers Who Own the Project
teachings.explanation: The most effective project management structure has PMs who take full ownership of every decision as if it were their own company, rather than acting as messengers or warm bodies on site. This requires systems, training, regular one-on-ones tied to their personal goals, and clear non-negotiable standards from day one.
teachings.quote: We like our project managers to own pretty much every decision as if it were their company which is structured differently than having an assistant on site.

teachings.explanation: Rather than a soft interview process, Erin presents candidates with a concrete scorecard of expectations upfront and asks them to reflect before proceeding. This filters for people who are truly aligned and prevents wasted training time on mismatched hires.
teachings.quote: I put this stuff in front of them like this is how this is what we expect boom can you do that I have a scorecard... look at this and we'll talk in a week and if you think you can do this we should keep talking if you don't it was nice meeting you.

teachings.concept: Trial and Error in Early Business Building
teachings.explanation: In the first zero to five years of building, it is more valuable to try different project types and business models than to over-optimize prematurely. Builders learn what they are good at and what serves clients best through direct experience, not hypotheticals.
teachings.quote: I just don't do hypotheticals I try it... zero to five years you know I was remodeling I was building new I was putting a spec home up I was carrying three kids around like I just tried to try try try and then at some point you're going to land where you serve people really well.

teachings.concept: Formal Project Close-Out as Boundary Setting
teachings.explanation: A structured closing walkthrough—covering homeowner education, a signed punch list, and explicit transition of responsibility—creates natural closure without awkwardness. It reduces ongoing client demands by clearly distinguishing completed contracted work from new issues.
teachings.quote: That meeting alone says closure without you having to be all awkward or weird about it... here's our closing meeting let's get you figured out.

teachings.concept: Being the Parent in Client Relationships
teachings.explanation: Clients will continue to escalate requests as long as there are no clear boundaries, similar to how children test limits. Builders must calmly and firmly establish what is done, what is new, and what falls under homeowner responsibility—modeling the behavior rather than resenting it.
teachings.quote: In a lot of cases we do have to be the parent and say you're going to be okay here's what you know here's how we're closing this out... that's what kids have taught me we have five kids like that's what they've taught me.

actionable_advice: Make a specific annual list of architects and designers you want to work with, research their values and client base, and pursue those relationships persistently over years, not months.
Offer to price a project for free for a target architect or feeder just to get in front of them and demonstrate how you think, even if you don't win the job.
Analyze every inbound project against your stated goals and decline work that does not move you toward those goals, even when your pipeline feels thin.
Limit each project manager to no more than two projects at a time to ensure full ownership and attention to detail.
Implement non-negotiable daily systems for PMs: daily action items, daily modification updates, and daily photo and video uploads to a shared system.
Before hiring a project manager, meet them on site and test their construction eye by asking open-ended questions like 'what do you notice in this room?' to assess competence.
Hold one-on-one meetings with team members every two weeks focused on their personal and professional goals, and align their role development accordingly.
Present PM candidates with a detailed scorecard of expectations before any job offer and give them time to decide if they can meet those standards.
At project close-out, conduct a formal homeowner education walkthrough covering how to shut off water, use the HVAC, check the electrical panel, and manage basic maintenance.
Create and distribute a written homeowner responsibility guide at project close that outlines what issues they should handle themselves versus call you for.
When clients continue calling about non-contracted issues after close-out, institute a service call fee to reduce unnecessary requests and reset expectations.
Clearly separate ongoing punch list items from new scope requests by labeling them as distinct projects and stating that project two begins only after project one is formally closed.
client_challenges: Erica is doing $150K-$200K remodels and struggling with subcontractors who take on multiple jobs but don't staff them adequately, leaving sites empty and projects stalled.
Erica is unsure how to break into higher-end custom home building when her experience base is smaller remodels and she has not yet built a ground-up custom home.
Erica and Beth experienced a period with no work in December and are concerned about business consistency.
Dave is scaling from 2-3 spec homes to 5-6 and is unsure whether to hire a superintendent, a second project manager, or some combination to manage the expanded workload.

Denise has struggled to find project managers who are willing to take full ownership and be available beyond standard hours, including losing a well-compensated PM of five years who ultimately didn't want the responsibility level.
Denise is in a market where architects and designers often design beyond client budgets and are difficult to partner with effectively on design-build projects.
Bennett is trying to decide whether to commit fully to a design-build model or remain open to standalone construction work, and is unsure how to define what he is scaling toward.

Sandy described a remodel project with a punch list that has dragged on for three months, with the client calling about electrical issues unrelated to the contracted scope, and needing a way to close out gracefully.
notable_quotes: On staying in the trenches: 'What makes a great builder is digging into the details staying in the details closing every single loop possible and just getting like staying in the trenches.'
On longevity in the business: 'If you make it past year 12 you're good.'

On taking on misaligned work: 'If you make it past if you make it past year 12 you're good... by showing up by doing things differently than other people if you can make anyone's job easier you just gained a ton of business.'
On knowing yourself as a builder: 'If you ask me to implement something I will climb a mountain backwards and roll down and it's going to happen so like I just know that that's where to play well.'
On spec building and paying subs to think: 'The business model I was using was I will pay more for subs to think on my behalf.'
On client management and boundary setting: 'We do have to be the parent and say you're going to be okay here's how we're closing this out... that's what kids have taught me.'
